stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]] + [[b]]\cdot\dfrac{x - ([[c]])}{\lvert x - ([[c]]) \rvert} \). Evaluate the right-hand limit \( \lim_{x \to [[c]]^{+}} f(x) \).
solution: For \( x > [[c]] \), \( \frac{x - [[c]]}{|x - [[c]]|} = 1 \), so \( f(x) \to [[a]] + [[b]] = [[answer]] \). (Approaching from the left the value would be \( [[a]] - [[b]] \), so the two-sided limit does not exist.)
distractor_rationales: This is the left-hand limit, where the quotient equals \( -1 \).
This ignores the jump term entirely.
This uses only the jump size and drops the constant \( a \).
This overshoots by adding the jump twice.
This is off by one from the correct one-sided limit.
This is off by one from the correct one-sided limit.

stem: The piecewise function \( f(x) = \begin{cases} [[a]]x + ([[b]]) & x < [[c]] \\ [[d]]x + ([[e]]) & x \ge [[c]] \end{cases} \) has a jump discontinuity at \( x = [[c]] \). Evaluate the left-hand limit \( \lim_{x \to [[c]]^{-}} f(x) \).
solution: For \( x < [[c]] \), \( f \) follows the first branch, so \( \lim_{x \to [[c]]^{-}} f(x) = [[a]]\cdot[[c]] + ([[b]]) = [[answer]] \). (The right-hand limit uses the second branch, \( [[d]]\cdot[[c]] + ([[e]]) \), which differs, confirming the jump.)
distractor_rationales: This is the right-hand limit, computed from the second branch instead of the first.
This flips the sign of the intercept in the correct branch.
This uses the second branch's formula with the wrong sign on its intercept.
This drops the multiplication by \( c \) in the first branch.
This is off by one from the correct one-sided limit.
This is off by one from the correct one-sided limit.
This is off by two from the correct one-sided limit.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]] + \sqrt{[[b]]\left(x - ([[c]])\right)} \). Since \( f \) is defined only for \( x \ge [[c]] \), evaluate the (one-sided) limit \( \lim_{x \to [[c]]^{+}} f(x) \).
solution: As \( x \to [[c]]^{+} \), the quantity \( [[b]](x-[[c]]) \to 0^{+} \), so \( \sqrt{[[b]](x-[[c]])} \to 0 \). Hence \( f(x) \to [[a]] = [[answer]] \). (The left-hand limit does not exist since \( f \) is undefined for \( x < [[c]] \), so this one-sided limit is the only limit that makes sense here.)
distractor_rationales: This adds the radicand's coefficient \( b \) as if the square root itself approached \( b \).
This subtracts \( b \) instead of recognizing the square-root term vanishes.
This reports only the coefficient \( b \), dropping the constant \( a \) entirely.
This negates the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

stem: The piecewise function \( f(x) = \begin{cases} [[a]]x + ([[b]]) & x < [[c]] \\ [[d]]x + ([[e]]) & x \ge [[c]] \end{cases} \) turns out to be continuous at \( x = [[c]] \) (the two pieces meet there). Evaluate \( \lim_{x \to [[c]]} f(x) \).
solution: Since both one-sided limits agree here, the two-sided limit exists and equals either branch evaluated at \( x = [[c]] \): \( [[a]]\cdot[[c]] + ([[b]]) = [[answer]] \) (which matches \( [[d]]\cdot[[c]] + ([[e]]) \) from the other branch, confirming continuity).
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign of the intercept in the first branch.
This drops the multiplication by \( c \) in the first branch.
This flips the sign of the intercept in the second branch.
This drops the multiplication by \( c \) in the second branch.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by two from the correct limit.
